GossipDissemination: Epidemic spread is fundamentally a monotone process: a node, once infected, never reverts. That single observation drives the entire modeling choice. The infected variable is a boolean function over Nodes, so the reachable state space is the powerset 2^|Nodes| minus the empty configuration -- patient zero is fixed at node 1 in Init. The Gossip(n,p) action requires that p already carries the infection and n does not, then flips n; this guard is what makes the spread inductively monotone, since no action ever clears a bit. Because pure monotone systems terminate at the all-infected fixpoint and would yield deadlock, a Reset action lets the model loop back to patient zero once everyone is sick, keeping the trace finite without introducing forgetting in the middle of a spread. The history counter shadows the cardinality of the infected set and is bumped on every Gossip; the safety conjunct history <= |InfectedSet|+1 then witnesses that the count tracks the set growth rather than diverging, giving an inductive handle on the monotonicity claim.
HashSetBounded: A bounded hash set is really just a finite subset of a fixed Universe with a capacity ceiling, so the spec compresses to a single state variable: contents, the current set. There is no need for a separate size counter because Cardinality(contents) is derivable, and there is no need for a hash bucket array because the abstraction is at the set-theoretic level rather than the storage level. Insert(x) is guarded twice -- the element must come from the legal Universe and the current cardinality must be strictly below K -- and these two guards together are exactly what keeps the strong invariant Bounded inductive: any successor state still has at most K elements drawn from Universe. Remove(x) demands x already be present, so the spec rules out spurious deletions that could otherwise satisfy the invariant vacuously. The state space is finite by construction because contents ranges over the powerset of Universe filtered to size at most K, which is a small combinatorial number for the modest constants chosen.
HeartbeatFailureDetector: Failure detection via heartbeats hinges on a separation of concerns between the sender and the observer, and that separation is exactly what the three state variables encode. fresh[n] is the in-flight bit owned by the sender, misses[n] is the consecutive-miss counter owned by the observer, and status[n] is the observer's current verdict; none of the three is derivable from the others, because a node can be alive-with-zero-misses and have no fresh beat (a tick is about to happen), or alive-with-some-misses and a fresh beat just arrived. The TickReceived action consumes a fresh beat and resets misses to zero, which is the rule that makes the alive verdict robust against transient delays; TickMissed only fires when fresh is false, so it cannot accidentally erase a beat in flight. Suspect requires misses to have already saturated to K, making the suspected->alive boundary crisp. The implication status[n]=suspected => misses[n]=K is the inductive heart of the safety claim: once suspected, the counter is pinned at the threshold and only an explicit Heartbeat+TickReceived sequence can rehabilitate the node.
HirschbergSinclair: The classical O(N log N) leader election protocol propagates probes in doubling rings and lets only the maximum id survive every phase. Modeling the actual probe messages would explode the state space, so the spec abstracts each process to a single integer phase[i] counting how far that id has advanced, with a separate leader register. The Advance(i) action is guarded by a tournament condition -- for every j greater than i, j's phase must be strictly less than i's -- which is logically wrong for a faithful Hirschberg-Sinclair (in reality the larger id wins, not the smaller), so the spec actually flips the convention and uses smaller-wins, which is consistent with the SafetyInv that requires every j to be at most the elected leader id. Election fires only when one process has reached MaxPhase alone, making the unique-leader property a direct consequence of the action's guard: any other process at the same phase would falsify the universal quantifier and block Elect. The Reset transition exists purely to recycle the system after a leader emerges, since otherwise the post-election states would be terminal.
IdempotencyKey: An idempotency-key server is best understood as a memo table on top of a side-effect counter, and that is exactly what the three variables encode. cache[k] holds the recorded result for key k or NONE if unseen, sideEffects counts the actual external effects performed, and observed[k] is the result the client most recently received. The interesting modeling decision is that FirstCall and Retry must NOT both be enabled for the same key in the same state: FirstCall demands cache[k]=NONE while Retry demands cache[k]#NONE, and this disjoint guarding is what makes the side-effect arithmetic inductive. Each FirstCall increments sideEffects exactly once and adds exactly one key to ServedKeys, so the equality sideEffects = Cardinality(ServedKeys) is preserved step by step; Retry touches neither side. The strong invariant observed[k] # NONE => observed[k] = cache[k] then captures the contractual promise of an idempotency key: every retry sees the same answer the first call produced, never a fresh computation.
IdempotentWrite: Deduplication at the server is fundamentally about distinguishing the multiset of received messages from the set of effects that have actually been applied, and the spec separates those two concepts cleanly. inFlight is the messages the network has delivered (a set, since the abstraction does not care how many times a duplicate has arrived), processed is the subset of ids whose effect has fired, and counter is the running count of fired effects. Send simply grows inFlight; Process performs a case split on whether the id has already been recorded, and only the unseen branch increments counter and adds to processed. Because that branch is the only writer of counter and the only writer of processed, and because each fires together, the conjunct counter = Cardinality(processed) is a pointwise step invariant. The companion processed \subseteq inFlight rules out spurious processing of ids that were never delivered, which would otherwise let an attacker forge effects without a corresponding network event.
JealousHusbands: The puzzle is a constraint-search problem dressed up as a transition system, and the spec leans into that by representing the world as exactly the partition (left, right) of the six People, plus the boat side. Couples are encoded by name convention rather than as an explicit relation, and Mate decodes the i-th letter into the partner's name; this keeps the state vector minimal because nothing about couple identity needs to live in the state itself. The crux of the modeling is the SafeBank predicate: a wife may share a bank with another husband only if her own husband is on the same bank, and this must hold on BOTH banks of the post-state, not just the bank the boat departs from -- otherwise the puzzle's signature failure mode (the wife left alone on a bank that suddenly contains a stranger husband) would slip through. The boat alternates with the L/R variable, and Loads(side) restricts cargo to one or two passengers, matching the two-seat boat. The puzzle is solvable iff some trace reaches left={} with the safety invariant maintained at every step.
JobScheduling: A retrying job scheduler is essentially a small finite automaton with a counter, and the spec lays out exactly four observable states plus a bounded retries register and an ever_run flag. The ever_run bit is the modeling decision worth dwelling on: without it the safety claim that a succeeded job has actually executed at least once cannot be expressed, because succeeded carries no other historical trace. Fail decrements the system back to queued and bumps retries, but only when retries < MaxRetries, so the loop is hard-bounded by construction; once retries hits MaxRetries the only enabled exit from running is Abandon, which moves to failed_terminal. The compound safety invariant ties three things together: retries never exceeds MaxRetries, succeeded implies the job actually ran, and failed_terminal implies it both ran and exhausted its retry budget. Each clause is the witness for one of the three meaningful end conditions, and together they make the terminal classification crisp enough that no path can sneak into failed_terminal without honoring the retry contract.
JwtSession: Token lifetimes are about the interaction between three independent timelines: when a token was minted, how its expiry was set, and how wall-clock time has advanced. The spec keeps all three explicit -- status, expiry, and now -- because collapsing any two would lose the ability to express refresh, which extends expiry without resetting now. Issue creates a token with expiry = now+2, Refresh resets expiry to now+2 only while the token is still alive (now < expiry), and Tick advances now and atomically transitions active to expired the moment now+1 reaches expiry. Revoke is an out-of-band kill switch that flips active to revoked and is one-way, captured by the monotone ever_revoked flag. The interesting safety conjunct is that an active status implies ever_issued AND now < expiry AND NOT ever_revoked: that triple closes every backdoor by which the model could declare a session valid -- a token that was never issued, one whose clock has caught up, or one that was killed. MaxTime bounds the now domain and keeps the trace finite.
KnightsTour: A knight's tour is a Hamiltonian-path search on the knight's-move graph, and the spec encodes exactly the two pieces of information that path-search needs: where the knight is now and which squares have been visited so far. The board is the 4x4 grid of pairs (1..N) x (1..N), and KnightMove tests the L-shape geometrically by demanding that the absolute coordinate differences are {1,2} in some order -- this is the entire definition of legality, with no precomputed move table. Step picks any square that satisfies KnightMove relative to the current position AND is not already in visited, then atomically moves there and records it; the no-revisit rule is enforced by the visited \notin guard rather than by a length check, which is the right granularity for safety. Done is a self-loop that fires when the knight has no legal unvisited successor, preventing a deadlock from being reported as a violation while still leaving the trace exactly at the dead-end. The cardinality bound visited \subseteq Squares /\ |visited| <= N*N gives an inductive ceiling that mirrors the Hamiltonian goal.
KvStore: A key-value store at the abstraction level of a single-process map is just a function from Keys to Vals augmented with a NONE marker for absent keys, so the spec uses a single state variable, store, and lifts NONE into the codomain rather than carrying a separate liveness set. Put unconditionally writes a value, Delete demands the key currently be present (so the operation is observable rather than vacuous), and Get is a pure observation that leaves the state untouched -- modeled explicitly so the action vocabulary mirrors a real KV API even though the action has no state effect. The TypeOK / Valid pair is deliberately weak: it only asserts that every key maps into Vals \cup {NONE}, which is the strongest invariant available when no consistency property is layered on top. The point of this base spec is to be the harness onto which transactional, replicated, or versioned variants are stacked.
LamportClock: Lamport's logical clock rule is one of the most elegant invariants in distributed systems: max(local, sent) + 1. The spec embeds it directly in Receive, where the receiver's new clock is computed from the message timestamp and its own current clock, then incremented. The two state variables -- per-process clock and a multiset of in-flight (sender, timestamp) pairs -- are exactly enough to express the rule, because the sender's full history is unnecessary once the timestamp has been minted. Send atomically increments the sender's clock and emits a message stamped with that NEW value, which is the convention that lets the safety conjunct \A m \in channel : m[2] <= clock[m[1]] hold inductively: the sender's clock is monotone, so any past timestamp it minted is bounded by its present clock. Local fires for non-message events and is the simplest possible witness that increments are not gated on communication. The MaxClock cap is purely for finite-state exploration and is enforced uniformly on every clock update, including the Receive max+1 expression, which is what keeps the channel-subset declaration well-formed.
LeaderLease: A lease grants temporary local-read authority and is built on the contract that no two leaders can hold overlapping windows on the same wall clock. The spec uses three variables: leader (which node, or NONE), leaseEnd (the wall time at which the current lease becomes invalid), and now (a bounded global clock). Elect is the linchpin: it can only fire when there is no current leader OR the current lease has already expired (now >= leaseEnd), and the disjunction is what prevents a successor from grabbing the role mid-window. LocalRead is a stutter action gated by the leader's identity AND the lease still being live, modeling that reads outside the window are illegal even though the leader variable still names a node. Tick advances now monotonically and never touches leader, so the only way for an expired lease to be revoked is the implicit one: the next Elect overwrites it. The strong invariant pinning leaseEnd >= LeaseLen whenever a leader exists rules out a degenerate state in which someone claims leadership without paying for a fresh window.
LeakyBucket: Traffic shaping with a leaky bucket boils down to two independent counters: how full the bucket currently is, and how many arrivals have been refused. The spec uses queue and dropped, both bounded to 0..K so the state space is small. Arrive is the only action that grows queue, and it is guarded by queue < K, which is the precise condition that makes the bucket bounded -- without that guard the invariant would not be inductive. Drop fires only when queue = K, which guarantees a lost-arrival counter that increments solely in the saturation regime; if it could fire below capacity it would lie about the shaper's behavior. Leak is the constant-rate drain that decrements queue whenever it is non-empty, and it carries no notion of time because the model is asynchronous step semantics rather than clocked. ResetDropped exists only to keep the dropped counter from running off to infinity in the unbounded model and lets the trace cycle back through the saturation regime indefinitely.
LightsOut: Lights Out is a Z/2 linear-algebra puzzle dressed as a transition system, but the spec keeps it concrete by storing the grid as a function from cells to {0,1}. The only action is Press, and its semantics are folded into a single function-update expression: for every cell d, the new value is Flip(grid[d]) if d belongs to Toggled(c) and grid[d] otherwise. Toggled(c) intersects each candidate neighbor with Cells, which is the spec's clean trick for handling boundary cases -- corners toggle three cells, edges four, the center five, all from a single uniform expression that does not need conditional branches. Because every press is its own inverse over Z/2, the reachable state space is at most 2^9 grids, and the safety invariant is purely structural: every cell holds 0 or 1. The puzzle's actual goal (reach the all-off configuration) is not part of safety because it is a reachability question, and the spec's Press is unguarded so any cell may be pressed at any time, mirroring the player's freedom.
LinkedListBounded: A bounded linked list is operationally just a sequence with a length cap, and the spec leans into that by representing the entire list as a single Seq variable rather than nodes-and-pointers. InsertFront prepends with the standard <<v>> \o list construction and is guarded by Len < K, which is the only line in the spec that enforces the bound. DeleteByValue is the more interesting action: it uses CHOOSE to pick the index of the first occurrence of v and then splices the sequence around that index using SubSeq, modeling the value-based deletion that real linked-list APIs expose. The InSeq guard prevents deletion of an absent value, which would otherwise leave the spec silently no-opping; demanding presence makes every delete observable. The Bounded invariant pins both Len and the elementwise membership in Vals, the second clause being non-vacuous because the operations only ever insert from Vals and never invent ghost values.
LruCache: An LRU cache is fundamentally a doubly-ordered structure where insertion order tracks recency, and the spec cleverly compresses both the eviction discipline and the recency tracking into a single Seq with the convention head=LRU, tail=MRU. Get on a hit is the recency-bump: remove the key from its current position and re-append at the tail, which is exactly the move that makes future accesses see it as freshest. Put has three branches that reveal the policy: hit (refresh, identical to Get), miss-with-room (append at tail), and miss-at-capacity (drop the head and append at tail). That third branch is the LRU eviction rule itself, and it is the only line in the spec where Tail(order) appears, making the eviction site syntactically traceable. The Distinct invariant is critical because the recency semantics depend on key uniqueness in the sequence -- if a key could appear twice the bump operation would be ill-defined. Combined with the length bound, Distinct gives a tight characterization of legal cache states.
MCSLock: An MCS lock is a queue-based mutual-exclusion primitive whose entire essence is fairness via FIFO, and the spec captures it with the smallest possible representation: a single sequence whose head is the current holder. Enqueue appends a process to the tail and is guarded by NOT InQueue, which encodes the real-world rule that a thread cannot be waiting twice. Release demands queue[1] = p, so only the head -- the genuine current holder -- may release, and the operation is a Tail that promotes the next waiter to the head atomically. The strong safety conjunct NoDup combined with the length bound |queue| <= |Procs| is what keeps the queue from growing pathologically: every process can be waiting at most once, so the queue length is naturally bounded by the population, and the head-uniqueness gives mutual exclusion as a corollary -- there is exactly one queue[1] in any state where the queue is non-empty.
MarkSweepGc: A tracing garbage collector is a two-phase contract -- mark transitively from roots, then free everything unmarked -- and the spec models exactly that contract over a fixed object chain where each object i points at i-1. Reachable is computed as a bounded fixpoint of the edge relation starting from rootEdge, and the bound NumObjects is sufficient because any path in the chain has at most that many hops. StartMark snapshots Reachable atomically into marked and transitions to the sweep phase, modeling stop-the-world GC; the snapshot is what makes the safety property inductive, because any subsequent mutator action would need to violate it. Sweep adds Objects \ marked to freed, which is the literal definition of mark-sweep. DropRoot is the mutator's contribution: it lowers the root edge between collections, deliberately stranding higher objects so the next GC cycle has work to do, and it discards the previous mark set so a fresh trace must be performed. The contract that reachable objects are never freed is what the spec is built to make checkable.
MazeRunner: Grid-maze navigation is fundamentally about a position plus a static wall set, and the spec keeps this minimal: the only state variable is pos, while the maze topology lives entirely in the constant Walls. Walls are encoded as unordered two-element sets of adjacent cells, which is the right representation because a wall belongs symmetrically to both sides and the membership test then becomes set equality rather than direction-aware lookup. Adjacent permits orthogonal moves of unit length, and NoWall does the membership check {c1,c2} \notin Walls. Step composes both predicates with the implicit destination-in-grid constraint provided by quantifying over Cells, so a single conjunction captures the legality of every move: in the grid, adjacent, and not blocked. The safety invariant is intentionally minimal -- pos always lies inside Cells -- because the more interesting wall-respecting property is enforced inductively by the action guards rather than carried as a separate state predicate.
MemoryFence: The whole point of MFENCE is to prove that after the instruction completes the thread's writes are globally visible, and the spec turns that contract into the inductive property fenced[t] => Len(sb[t]) = 0. Three variables are needed because each carries information no other can: sb is the per-thread FIFO of pending stores, fenced is the boolean witness that the thread is currently in a fence-clean state, and issued bounds the total stores per thread so the trace is finite. Issue appends to the local buffer and clears fenced, modeling that any new in-flight write breaks the post-fence invariant. Drain consumes the head of the buffer asynchronously and only sets fenced back to TRUE if this drain emptied the buffer, which is the precise place where the invariant could be re-established without an explicit fence. MFence itself is enabling-style: it cannot fire until the buffer has already drained to empty, which mirrors how a real x86 program counter blocks at the fence instruction until the store buffer is observed clean. Idle prevents deadlock once issuance is exhausted.
MergeRequest: Code review workflows are a small finite state machine with a numeric guard, and the spec encodes both pieces literally. The state alphabet is draft, ready, approved, merged, closed, and the numeric guard is approver_count compared against RequiredApprovals. The cleverness is in Approve: it always increments approver_count but only flips status to approved when the post-increment count meets the threshold; otherwise the status remains ready. This conditional update is what allows the spec to faithfully model the gradual accumulation of approvals without inventing an intermediate state per increment. Merge then re-checks approver_count >= RequiredApprovals, even though that should already hold by construction, which makes the merged-implies-quorum invariant inductive on its own without relying on cross-action reasoning. The ever_ready ghost flag is necessary because merged hides the fact that the MR was promoted out of draft, and without that breadcrumb the invariant merged => ever_ready could not be expressed.
MesiCache: MESI exists specifically to add an Exclusive state between Shared and Modified so that an Exclusive owner can upgrade silently without a bus transaction, and the spec preserves that distinction in its action vocabulary. SilentUpgrade fires only on E and is the only action whose semantics rely on E being distinct from S. BusRead computes the post-state for every core in a single function-update: the requester becomes E if no other core has the line and S otherwise, while existing M or E owners are demoted to S because the read makes the line shared. BusReadX is the write-miss action and atomically invalidates every other core's copy, which is the line that makes the invariant SingleModified hold. The OwnerExclusive conjunct is the heart of the safety story: any core in M or E forces every other core to be I, so M-with-S, E-with-S, and M-with-E configurations are unreachable. Together with SingleModified and SingleExclusive, this gives the full MESI sole-owner contract.
MissionariesCannibals: The classic puzzle is a two-counter bank-balance problem with a sharp safety predicate, and the spec encodes the two banks as four scalar counts plus the boat side rather than as sets. That choice matters: counts let SideSafe be expressed as a tiny arithmetic formula c <= m (or no missionaries present) instead of a set-cardinality comparison. MoveLR and MoveRL each existentially choose the boat's cargo as a (dm, dc) pair with dm + dc in 1..2, then enforce four conjuncts: the source bank actually has the people, the destination updates correctly, and BOTH banks satisfy SideSafe in the post-state. Checking both banks is essential -- a configuration that is safe only on the departure side would let cannibals overwhelm the missionaries on the arrival side as the boat lands. The conservation conjuncts mL + mR = N and cL + cR = N are inductive because every action transfers people without creating or destroying them, so the trace stays inside a finite slice of the integer lattice.
Monitor: A monitor with one condition variable splits its population into two disjoint groups: at most one process holding the lock and any number of processes parked on the wait queue, and the spec uses two set-valued state variables to express exactly that. Enter requires the inside set to be empty AND the entrant not to be currently waiting, which together prevent both double-occupancy and the bug where a waiter spuriously re-enters without going through the wakeup path. Wait is the action that releases the lock and parks the caller; it atomically empties inside and adds the caller to waiting, so there is no instant in which the same process could appear in both sets. SignalAndExit captures Hoare semantics: the signaler exits the monitor and the woken waiter immediately takes ownership, modeled by choosing some q from waiting and assigning inside to {q}. WakeWaiter is a separate action that fires when the monitor is free but waiters remain, guaranteeing forward progress when no holder exists to do the signaling. The conjunct inside cap waiting = {} is the invariant the action guards collectively maintain.
MsiCache: MSI is the simpler ancestor of MESI and lives by exactly one rule: a Modified line is held by exactly one core and excludes every other valid copy. The spec encodes core states with a single function variable state and lets four actions move it through the legal transitions. WriteHit on a Shared line is the most semantically loaded transition: it must be a single atomic step that flips the local copy to M AND invalidates every other core's copy in one functional update, because any non-atomic version would have a window in which two cores were simultaneously M-and-S, falsifying ModifiedExcludesShared. BusReadX has the same atomic-invalidation structure for the cold-write case. BusRead is the gentler read-miss: any other M-owner downgrades to S in the same step the requester acquires its S copy, modeling the writeback. Evict moves any non-I copy back to I and is unconditional, modeling silent capacity eviction. Together SingleWriter and ModifiedExcludesShared make the M-state the unique-owner state at every reachable configuration.
MultilevelFeedbackQueue: MLFQ is interesting because the scheduler's policy is encoded as a monotone level descent: a task starts at the highest priority and only ever moves down the priority ladder, never back up. The spec captures this with a per-task level function, a per-task used quantum counter, and a single running task identifier (or NoTask sentinel). Schedule picks a task whose level matches LowestLevelWithReady -- the smallest L for which a ready task exists -- which is the spec's encoding of priority. Tick advances used while it remains strictly below Quantum, leaving level untouched; Demote fires only at the quantum boundary, capping level at 2 and resetting used. The interesting safety subtlety is that LevelInv pins used to 0..(Quantum-1) -- never Quantum itself -- because Demote always atomically resets used at the moment it would otherwise saturate. IOBlock is the voluntary yield that keeps the task at its current level, modeling that a well-behaved task that blocks for I/O does not get punished by demotion.
Multiset: A bounded multiset is just a function from a Universe to capped natural numbers, and the spec carries exactly that single state variable. Each element's multiplicity lives independently in 0..K, so the joint state space is |Universe|^(K+1) but factored cleanly across elements. Add bumps the count for one element, guarded by counts[x] < K so the per-element cap is enforced inductively rather than only declared in the invariant. Remove decrements but only when the count is strictly positive, which is what makes the spec faithful to a real multiset rather than a signed one -- there are no negative counts and no clamping at zero hidden inside the action body, because the guard rules out the underflow case entirely. The Bounded conjunct expresses the cap as a per-element membership in 0..K, and TypeOK promotes counts to a function on Universe so that any quantification over elements is well-typed.
Mutex: A mutex is the simplest possible mutual-exclusion primitive: a single owner that may be free or held by exactly one process. The spec models this with a single set variable owner that is either empty or a singleton, which is more permissive in type than a process-or-NONE encoding but tighter under the safety constraint Cardinality(owner) <= 1. Lock fires only when owner = {}, which is the linchpin of mutual exclusion -- two processes cannot both find the mutex free in the same state and so cannot both succeed in becoming the next owner. TryLockSucceed and TryLockFail split the try-lock semantics into two distinct actions whose disjunction covers both outcomes, which is more honest than a non-deterministic single action because it makes the failure case observable in the trace. Unlock requires owner = {p}, so only the actual holder can release; no other process can mistakenly relinquish a lock it does not own. The cardinality bound is inductive because no action can grow owner beyond a single element.
MutexWithTimeout: Adding a timeout to a mutex changes the protocol from blocking to bounded-effort, and the spec captures the additional semantics with a per-process attempts counter and a timed_out program-counter state. Each process has a small program counter pc[i] cycling through ncs, trying, cs, and timed_out, with a per-process attempts in 0..MaxAttempts. Spin is the failure-without-progress action: the lock is held by someone else and attempts is below the cap, so the counter increments without changing pc. Timeout fires only when attempts has saturated, transitioning the process to timed_out without acquiring the lock. The crucial mutual-exclusion conjunct is the universal that says any two distinct processes cannot both be in cs simultaneously, and it remains inductive because Acquire is the only action that enters cs and it requires lock = NoHolder. The complementary lock # NoHolder => pc[lock] = cs ties the lock register to the actual program-counter state of its owner, ruling out the bug where a process is recorded as the holder but is no longer in its critical section.
MvccSnapshot: Snapshot isolation is defined by exactly two guarantees: every transaction reads from the version that was current when it began, and concurrent writers conflict at commit time. The spec encodes the first by snapping curVer into snap[t] in Begin, and the second by demanding snap[t] = curVer in CommitWrite -- if anyone else committed a write since this transaction began, the equality fails and CommitWrite is disabled. The Abort action is enabled in the complementary case (snap[t] # curVer), modeling the loser of the write race learning about its conflict and stepping down. CommitRead has no version-equality guard because read-only transactions never observe write conflicts under SI; they record their snapshot version as their commit version and exit cleanly. The monotone curVer counter and the per-transaction commitVer together let any external observer reconstruct the global commit order, and the strong invariant pinning commit versions to the moment of CommitWrite gives an inductive handle on serialization order without storing a separate history.
NQueensPlacement: Column-by-column N-queens turns a constraint-satisfaction problem into a bounded-length sequence-building exercise, and the spec leans into that by representing the partial placement as a Seq[Rows] whose i-th element is the row chosen for column i. PlaceCol existentially picks a row for the next column and then re-checks NonAttacking on the entire augmented sequence, which is the spec's clean way of pushing inductive maintenance to a single predicate evaluation rather than carrying separate row, diagonal, and anti-diagonal sets. The NonAttacking definition compares every prior column to every later one with both the row equality test (column attack) and the AbsDiff equality test (diagonal attack); together those exhaust the queen's threat geometry, since column attacks are impossible by construction. col is a redundant counter pinned to Len(placement)+1 by the SafetyInv conjunct Len(placement) = col - 1, which makes the spec's shape inductively obvious without inferring it from the sequence each time. Done idles when the board is full or no row extension is legal.
Numa: A two-socket NUMA model is fundamentally about delayed cross-socket visibility, and the spec separates per-socket memory from in-flight propagation traffic with two functions, mem and pending. The interesting modeling decision is that LocalWrite is gated by an all-pending-clear precondition: \A r in Sockets \ {s} : pending[r] = 0 forbids overlapping writes from different sockets. This is the choice that makes the eventual-consistency invariant clean -- without it the model would have to reason about racing propagations and would need a serialization layer the spec deliberately avoids. Propagate is asynchronous and may fire any time a pending slot is non-zero, modeling unbounded but finite cross-socket latency. The QuiescentAgreement conjunct is the contract: whenever every pending slot is zero, every socket's mem agrees with every other, which captures the eventual-consistency promise that NUMA memory delivers once all writes have settled. Idle parks the system once issuance is exhausted and propagation has quiesced.
OAuth2Flow: OAuth 2.0's authorization-code flow exists to make every step's prerequisites auditable, so the spec is built around a chain of monotone witness flags -- ever_consented, ever_code, ever_token -- that record whether each gate has historically been crossed. The current phase variable steps through start, consented, code_issued, token_issued, resource_access (or branches to denied), but phase alone cannot witness the chain because terminal states would lose the history. The witness flags carry that history forward, so the safety claim resource_access => (ever_token AND ever_code AND ever_consented) becomes a simple conjunction of monotone facts and is inductive because each witness is set in exactly one action and is never cleared. The denied branch is the complementary path: it forks from start without ever setting ever_token, so the conjunct denied => NOT ever_token holds trivially because Deny disables every subsequent token-issuing action.
Onboarding: The onboarding pipeline is a strictly linear sequence of four gates -- signup, email verification, profile completion, terms acceptance -- and the spec uses one phase variable plus four monotone history flags to make the gating auditable. Each transition advances phase exactly one slot and sets the corresponding ever_* flag, never clearing any prior flag, so the witness chain grows monotonically along any trace. The non-trivial design choice is that each Verify/Complete/Accept action is guarded by the EXACT predecessor phase rather than by a presence test on the prior history flag; this means the linear order is enforced both temporally (you cannot reach email_verified without first being signed_up) and historically (the ever_signup flag is necessarily true once email_verified is reached). The compound SafetyInvariant decomposes into one implication per phase, each requiring the union of all earlier history flags. Because each ever_* flag is set in exactly one action and never cleared, every implication is preserved step by step.
Once: The once-only initialization pattern -- think pthread_once or std::call_once -- has a single hard contract: the underlying action must execute exactly one time regardless of how many concurrent callers race for it. The spec captures the contract with a runs counter pinned to 0..1 and three observable states: Pending, Running, Done. StartFirst is the only action that increments runs, and it requires state = Pending; once it fires, state moves to Running and Pending becomes unreachable until the next reset, so a second StartFirst is statically impossible. WaitCall and LateCall are the two ways concurrent callers observe the in-progress and completed action, and neither touches runs -- they only record the caller in callers. LeaveAll is the recycling action that empties callers once everyone has observed Done; it does NOT reset runs, which is the spec's deliberate way of saying that the action remains executed-once for the lifetime of the trace. The OnceSafe conjuncts pin runs to 0 in Pending and to 1 in Running or Done, making the at-most-once invariant trivially inductive.
OptimisticConcurrency: Validation-based optimistic concurrency control checks at commit time that nothing a transaction observed has changed in the meantime, and the spec encodes that by capturing per-transaction read-sets as a function from Keys to the version observed for each key. Read records the version of one key into readSet[t][k], and Commit's first conjunct -- \A k in Keys : readSet[t][k] = version[k] -- is the validation step: any key whose version drifted since the read invalidates the entire transaction. The cleverness is using the entire-key universal even for keys the transaction never read; uninitialized read-set entries default to 0, which only matches the global version[k] when the key has not been written either, so unread keys impose no constraint until somebody else writes them. The write set W is chosen existentially at commit time, and version is bumped only for keys in W whose current value is below MaxVersion. commitVer[t] snapshots the post-commit version vector for the transaction's record, providing an audit trail for serialization order.
OptimisticLockVersion: Compare-and-swap on a versioned record is a two-phase pattern: read the version into a local snapshot, do the work, then attempt to write under the assumption that the version has not moved. The spec compresses both phases into the per-writer status machine idle -> reading -> committed/aborted. Read snapshots version into snap[w] and transitions to reading, modeling the checkpoint that future commit attempts will compare against. CommitOk fires only when snap[w] = version, atomically incrementing version and marking the writer committed; this is the CAS, and its enabling condition is the source of inductiveness for the whole spec. CommitFail is the complementary action -- snap[w] # version -- that retires the writer as aborted without touching version. The strong invariant snap[w] <= version holds because version is monotone and snap was sampled at some prior point, while status[w] = committed => snap[w] < version captures that any successful commit took the version strictly past the snapshot it observed, ruling out two writers both succeeding on the same prior snapshot.
OrderLifecycle: The order workflow is the canonical paid-then-shipped-then-delivered chain with an early-cancel branch, and the spec uses three monotone history flags to make the cross-state safety claims expressible as state predicates. The interesting modeling decision is that ever_cancelled is recorded but only ever_paid and ever_shipped feed the delivered-implication: a delivered order must have been both paid and shipped at some prior point, and this is enforced inductively because Pay and Ship are the only setters of those flags and Deliver requires status = shipped, which can only be reached via Ship. The complementary cancelled => NOT ever_shipped invariant works because Cancel is guarded to fire only from {created, paid}, never from shipped, so any state in which ever_shipped became true is one in which Cancel is no longer enabled. Done is a stutter on the two terminal states, preventing them from being misreported as deadlocks while still leaving the trace at the accepting end.
OrderedMulticast: Total-order multicast via a sequencer hinges on a single global numbering source: the sequencer assigns monotone integers and every receiver delivers strictly in that order. The spec captures this with three variables -- a counter seq, a sequence broadcast that records the assignment history (where broadcast[i] = i is a tautological pin used to assert the assignment is dense and in-order), and a per-receiver delivered that records how far each receiver has consumed. Assign bumps seq by one and appends the new number to broadcast in the same step, so the conjunct Len(broadcast) = seq is preserved. Deliver(r) is guarded by delivered[r] < seq, which is the only way order is enforced -- a receiver cannot leap ahead of an unassigned slot, and because deliveries advance one step at a time the delivered prefix is necessarily a prefix of broadcast. The conjunct delivered[r] <= seq is the inductive shadow of that prefix property: every receiver lags or matches the sequencer, and they all therefore agree on whatever has been delivered.
OutboxPattern: The transactional outbox pattern exists to bridge a local DB transaction with an asynchronous downstream publish, and the safety contract is that the published set is always a subset of what has been durably committed locally. The spec encodes the world with three sets: dbRows (the application's own data), outbox (the staged outbound messages), and published (what the relay has emitted). WriteLocal is the local transaction: it adds m to BOTH dbRows and outbox in a single step, which is the modeling of atomicity that the pattern's correctness depends on -- if the two updates were separable, the relay could observe an outbox entry without a corresponding committed row. Publish is the relay's action and is guarded by m \in outbox, so it cannot run ahead of the local commit. The invariant outbox = dbRows is the explicit witness for the atomic-write contract, and published \subseteq outbox is the explicit witness for the publish-after-commit contract; together they rule out the dual-write anomaly that the pattern was invented to prevent.
PageReplacementClock: The Clock (second-chance) algorithm is interesting because it avoids the need for a true LRU stack by using a single moving hand and a per-frame reference bit, and the spec encodes that hardware-friendly structure faithfully. Three variables -- frame, ref, and hand -- carry exactly the data the algorithm reads and writes, no more. Hit refreshes ref to 1 for the resident frame holding the requested page, modeling the access bit set by the MMU. MissFreeSlot installs a new page in any empty frame and sets its ref to 1, leaving the hand alone because no replacement decision was needed. MissEvict is the heart of the algorithm: it inspects the frame under the hand and either evicts (if ref=0) or clears the bit and advances (if ref=1), in either case bumping hand by (hand+1) mod K. The two-armed conditional inside MissEvict is the second-chance rule itself, and the modular arithmetic on hand is what keeps the trace bounded to a circular buffer. Cardinality(Resident) <= K and the no-duplicates conjunct together pin the resident-set semantics independently of how the hand moves.
PageReplacementLru: True LRU page replacement requires explicit access-recency information for every resident page, and the spec compresses both the resident set and the recency order into a single sequence with the convention head=LRU, tail=MRU. RemoveAll is a recursive helper that strips every occurrence of a page from the sequence -- it works for the general case even though the NoDuplicates invariant guarantees there is at most one occurrence at any reachable state, and that defensive design lets the recursion be simple. Hit removes the page from its current position and re-appends at the tail, which is the move that makes future references see it as freshest. MissWithSpace appends at the tail because the new page is by definition just-accessed, and MissEvict drops the head and appends -- the head being dropped is the genuinely least-recently-used page because the sequence's leftmost element is the one that has spent the longest stretch without a Hit. Bounded plus NoDuplicates gives the LRU contract: the resident set has size at most K and contains every page exactly once.
